user_content: I spent around 70 hours playing Assassin's Creed Odyssey, and I found the combat to be engaging, but not overly complex. If The Witcher 3's combat is more challenging, I'm willing to give it a try. Can you recommend any tips or strategies for a smooth transition from Odyssey's combat to The Witcher 3's?
I'm thinking of trying out some of the other games you mentioned, like Red Dead Redemption 2 or Horizon Zero Dawn. Can you tell me more about the storytelling and atmosphere in those games?

I'm looking for some recommendations for indie games similar to Hyper Light Drifter, which took me 5 hours to finish, by the way. I loved its atmospheric soundtrack and beautiful visuals. Can you suggest some games with similar qualities?

I'm looking for some recommendations on new games to play. I've been playing a lot of action-adventure games lately, like The Last of Us Part II, which I completed on hard difficulty and it took me 30 hours to finish. Do you have any suggestions for similar games that I might enjoy?

I'm glad you mentioned Uncharted 4's cinematic approach. It's definitely one of the game's strongest aspects. By the way, I've been thinking about my gaming habits lately, and I realized that I spent around 30 hours playing The Last of Us Part II on hard difficulty, which was a really challenging but rewarding experience. Do you think Uncharted 4's difficulty level is comparable to The Last of Us Part II's hard mode?
I'm trying to find some new indie games to play on my Switch. Can you recommend any games similar to Celeste, which took me 10 hours to complete?

I'm interested in What Remains of Edith Finch. Since it's a shorter game, I'd like to know more about its replay value. Are there multiple endings or secrets to discover that would encourage me to play through the game again?

I'm interested in exploring more indie games with strong storytelling and character development. Are there any games that you would recommend that have a similar atmosphere and tone to What Remains of Edith Finch?
I'm looking for some recommendations on games similar to The Last of Us Part II. By the way, I just finished it on normal difficulty and it took me 25 hours to complete.

That's really interesting. I didn't know it took them 5-6 years to develop the game. I can imagine the emotional storytelling part was a challenge, considering how emotional the game can be. By the way, have you got any recommendations for games with similar emotional storytelling, but maybe in a different setting or genre?

I think I'll check out ResetEra and Reddit's r/Games. I've heard good things about both communities, and they seem like great places to discuss games with other players. Thanks for the recommendations! By the way, I'm also interested in learning more about the gaming industry and staying up-to-date with the latest news and developments. Are there any good websites or YouTube channels you'd recommend for gaming news and analysis?

I think I'll start with Polygon and Kotaku for written articles, and then check out some YouTube channels like GameSpot and Jimquisition. I've heard great things about Jim Sterling's in-depth analysis and critique of the gaming industry. I might also give the Game Informer Show podcast a listen, as I've enjoyed their magazine in the past.

By the way, I've been thinking about getting a new gaming keyboard. Do you have any recommendations for good gaming keyboards, or any specific features I should look for?
I'm leaning towards The Witcher 3, but I'm worried about the combat being too challenging. How does it compare to Assassin's Creed Odyssey's combat in terms of difficulty and complexity?
I'm looking for games with strong narratives, so I think I'll check out Uncharted 4 and God of War. I've heard great things about them. Have you got any info on their storytelling and characters?

I'm really excited to dive into these games. I've heard that the storytelling in Uncharted 4 is heavily influenced by films, and I'm curious to see how that translates to a gaming experience. Do you think the game's cinematic approach will make the story feel more immersive and engaging?

So, I'm curious about the other games you mentioned earlier, like Horizon Zero Dawn and God of War. How do their difficulty levels compare to Uncharted 4 and The Last of Us Part II? Are they more or less challenging?
